questions.explanation: Glycine has hydrogen as its R-group, so the alpha carbon is not chiral.

questions.questionText: Which amino acid contains an indole ring in its side chain?
questions.explanation: Tryptophan has a bulky, bicyclic indole side chain.

questions.questionText: The peptide bond is characterized by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The partial double bond nature restricts rotation around the C-N bond.

questions.questionText: The alpha-helix and beta-pleated sheet represent which level of protein structure?
questions.explanation: Secondary structure involves local folding stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms.

questions.questionText: Which amino acid disrupts the alpha-helix due to its strict cyclic structure?
questions.explanation: Proline lacks an amide hydrogen to donate to an H-bond and causes a 'kink'.

questions.questionText: The isoelectric point (pI) of a protein is the pH at which:
questions.optionB: It moves fastest in an electric field
questions.optionC: It has no net electrical charge
questions.explanation: At the pI, the net charge is zero (zwitterion predominates).

questions.questionText: Disulfide bonds, which stabilize tertiary structure, are formed between residues of:
questions.explanation: Cysteine thiol groups can oxidize to form a covalent disulfide cystine bond.

questions.questionText: Hemoglobin is an example of a protein with:
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin is a tetramer composed of four polypeptide subunits.

questions.questionText: In collagen, every third amino acid is typically:
questions.explanation: Glycine's small size allows the tight packing of the collagen triple helix.

questions.questionText: Which amino acid acts as a major physiological buffer due to its pKa being close to physiological pH?
questions.explanation: The imidazole side chain of histidine has a pKa around 6.0.

questions.questionText: In Chemistry of Proteins, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Biochemistry texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Biochemistry references for Chemistry of Proteins. Subject: Biochemistry. Chapter: Chemistry of Proteins.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Chemistry of Proteins scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Biochemistry. Chapter: Chemistry of Proteins.
